content:
# Hebrews Chapter 13 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/HEB_012_page_002.jpg

1. Let brotherly love continue.

2. Do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels unawares.

3. Remember those who are in prison, as though in prison with them, and those who are mistreated, since you also are in the body.

4. Let marriage be held in honor among all, and let the marriage bed be undefiled, for God will judge the sexually immoral and adulterous.

5. Keep your life free from love of money, and be content with what you have, for he has said, "I will never leave you nor forsake you."

6. So we can confidently say, "The Lord is my helper; I will not fear; what can man do to me?"

7. Remember your leaders, those who spoke to you the word of God. Consider the outcome of their way of life, and imitate their faith.

8. Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever.

9. Do not be led away by diverse and strange teachings, for it is good for the heart to be strengthened by grace, not by foods, which have not benefited those devoted to them.

10. We have an altar from which those who serve the tent have no right to eat.

11. For the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy places by the high priest as a sacrifice for sin are burned outside the camp.

12. So Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood.

13. Therefore let us go to him outside the camp and bear the reproach he endured.

14. For here we have no lasting city, but we seek the city that is to come.

15. Through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of lips that acknowledge his name.

16. Do not neglect to do good and to share what you have, for such sacrifices are pleasing to God.

17. Obey your leaders and submit to them, for they are keeping watch over your souls, as those who will have to give an account. Let them do this with joy and not with groaning, for that would be of no advantage to you.

18. Pray for us, for we are sure that we have a clear conscience, desiring to act honorably in all things.

19. I urge you the more earnestly to do this in order that I may be restored to you the sooner.

20. Now may the God of peace who brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, the great shepherd of the sheep, by the blood of the eternal covenant,

21. equip you with everything good that you may do his will, working in us that which is pleasing in his sight, through Jesus Christ, to whom be glory forever and ever. Amen.

22. I appeal to you, brothers, bear with my word of exhortation, for I have written to you briefly.

23. You should know that our brother Timothy has been released, with whom I shall see you if he comes soon.

24. Greet all your leaders and all the saints. Those who come from Italy send you greetings.

25. Grace be with all of you.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 13 shifts from theology to practice — though even its practical instructions carry deep theological roots. The "outside the camp" theme (vv.11-14) connects the sin offering's disposal to Jesus' crucifixion site to the community's social location. The benediction (vv.20-21) is the only explicit resurrection reference in Hebrews, and the phrase "blood of the eternal covenant" encapsulates the entire letter's argument in four words.

**Verse 8 — "The same yesterday and today and forever":** The chapter's theological center. Placed between "remember your leaders" (v.7) and "do not be led away by strange teachings" (v.9), Christ's immutability is the criterion for evaluating both past leaders and present teachings.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.9 — "not by foods" vs. "not from rules about food"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not by foods, in which those walking were not profited" | ou brōmasin en hois ouk ōphelēthēsan hoi peripatountes |
| LOGOS | "not by foods, which have not benefited those devoted to them" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "not from rules about food, which don't help those who follow them" | "Rules about food" for brōmasin |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. brōmasin means "foods" — the noun, not "rules about food." The author refers to foods themselves (likely food laws or ritual meals), not rules about food. PNEUMA adds an abstraction layer: the issue is the foods, not the rules governing them.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### v.14 — "we have no lasting city here" vs. "this world is not our permanent home"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we have no lasting city here, but we seek the one to come" | ou gar echomen hōde menousan polin alla tēn mellousan epizētoumen |
| LOGOS | "here we have no lasting city, but we seek the city that is to come" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "this world is not our permanent home; we are looking forward to a home yet to come" | "World" for implicit location; "home" for polin (city) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The author says polis (city), not "home" or "world." The city metaphor is deliberate and connects to the "city with foundations" (11:10), the "heavenly Jerusalem" (12:22), and the "city to come" (13:14). PNEUMA's "home" domesticates the public/political metaphor into a private/domestic one.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA changes city to home.

---

### v.20 — "by the blood of the eternal covenant" vs. "ratified an eternal covenant with his blood"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "by the blood of the eternal covenant" | en haimati diathēkēs aiōniou |
| LOGOS | "by the blood of the eternal covenant" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "ratified an eternal covenant with his blood" | Adds "ratified"; restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek en haimati diathēkēs aiōniou modifies the verb "brought up" — God brought Jesus from the dead "by/in the blood of the eternal covenant." PNEUMA restructures into a separate clause ("ratified an eternal covenant with his blood"), making it a second action rather than a descriptor of the first. The blood of the covenant is the means of resurrection, not a separate act of ratification.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. Whether the blood describes the means of resurrection (LOGOS) or a separate act of ratification (PNEUMA) affects the relationship between cross and resurrection.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA changes metaphors | 1 | "Home" for "city" (v.14) |
| PNEUMA adds abstraction | 1 | "Rules about food" for "foods" (v.9) |
| PNEUMA restructures clauses | 1 | Separate ratification for instrumental blood (v.20) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 13's most significant disagreement is v.20 — the relationship between the blood of the eternal covenant and the resurrection. The author says God brought Jesus from the dead "by the blood of the eternal covenant" — connecting the cross directly to the resurrection as cause and means. PNEUMA separates them into two actions (raising and ratifying), losing the causal connection. The chapter's other notable issue is v.14's "home" for "city" — a recurring pattern where PNEUMA domesticizes political/public language.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
